articles.title: How to Structure and Make a Data Team Truly Work!
articles.excerpt: In a world where quick and accurate decisions make all the difference, data teams have ceased to be a luxury and have become an essential engine of competitiveness. It's not just about collecting data or building beautiful dashboards.
articles.body:
- Report this article

## Otávio R.

In a world where quick and accurate decisions make all the difference, data teams have ceased to be a luxury and have become an essential engine of competitiveness. It's not just about collecting data or building beautiful dashboards. It's about creating a sustainable flow of transforming data into real value for the business. In this journey, three profiles emerge with complementary roles: Data Engineers, Data Analysts, and Data Scientists. Understanding how each contributes, and especially how they collaborate, is fundamental to creating a structure that generates lasting impact.

## The Foundation:

> Why is a data team strategic?

Before we talk about roles, it's important to understand why seriously investing in a data team is a strategic step. Data, in isolation, is worthless. It is the ability to translate this data into information, knowledge, and ultimately wisdom, that transforms companies. This chain is known as the DIKW pyramid, which shows how raw data evolves into intelligent decisions when well-structured and interpreted. Without a dedicated team, organizations end up with fragmented projects, lack of governance, and analyses that never impact strategic decisions. This is where the need to combine people, processes, and technology comes in.

## The roles that make up an effective team:

## 1. Data Engineer

> The architect of the infrastructure!

Data Engineers are the ones who build and maintain the organization's data backbone. They not only extract data from different sources but also ensure that this flow operates reliably, scalably, and with the quality standards that other areas require. A robust pipeline means less rework, less firefighting, and more focus on generating value. Key points of the role:

Design and implement data ingestion and transformation pipelines.Ensure that data is placed in reliable, versioned, and traceable formats for consumers.Scale the architecture as the company grows and needs evolve.

- Design and implement data ingestion and transformation pipelines.

- Ensure that data is placed in reliable, versioned, and traceable formats for consumers.

- Scale the architecture as the company grows and needs evolve.

Instead of simply “running jobs,” a good engineer thinks about robustness, automation, and governance from the start.

## 2. Data Analyst

> Translator between numbers and business.

Data Analysts are the ones who transform numbers into useful narratives for business areas. They stay close to real everyday questions: “Why has the churn rate increased?”, “Which customer segments are growing the most?”, “Which KPIs are out of standard?”. This role is essential because this is where data stops being technical and becomes useful and understandable for managers and stakeholders. Typical contributions:

Building dashboards and reports that explain phenomena.Interpreting data in light of strategic objectives.Defining and tracking metrics that tell a story for the business.

- Building dashboards and reports that explain phenomena.

- Interpreting data in light of strategic objectives.

- Defining and tracking metrics that tell a story for the business.

An excellent analyst not only masters SQL and BI tools but also knows how to tell stories that guide real decisions.

## 3. Data Scientist

## Recommended by LinkedIn

> Explorer and modeler of future scenarios.

While the analyst answers “what happened and why,” the data scientist seeks to understand what might happen and how to act on it. This role is often associated with Machine Learning techniques, advanced statistics, and predictive modeling. But it goes far beyond that; a good data scientist translates business problems into testable hypotheses and models that add continuous value.

Main activities:

Building and validating predictive or prescriptive models.Deep exploration of patterns that are not visible in descriptive analyses.Collaborating with engineers to put models into production.

- Building and validating predictive or prescriptive models.

- Deep exploration of patterns that are not visible in descriptive analyses.

- Collaborating with engineers to put models into production.

The great differentiator of this role is the ability to anticipate scenarios, something that, when done well, transforms into a competitive advantage.

## How do these roles interconnect in practice?

There is no real data team without collaboration. Data engineers should not work in the dark, but rather aligned with what analysts and scientists need: reliable, available, and understandable data. On the other hand, analysts and scientists cannot expect their work to be efficient if they spend time cleaning data or rebuilding pipelines all the time. The ideal model follows a continuous cycle:

Clear definition of the business problem.Always coming from the area that demands results.Data engineering.Pipelines that ensure quality and governance.Exploratory analysis and reports.Transforming data into immediate insights.Advanced and predictive modeling.Anticipating future possibilities.Feedback and iteration.Refining both the data and the questions.

- Clear definition of the business problem.Always coming from the area that demands results.

- Data engineering.Pipelines that ensure quality and governance.

- Exploratory analysis and reports.Transforming data into immediate insights.

- Advanced and predictive modeling.Anticipating future possibilities.

- Feedback and iteration.Refining both the data and the questions.

## Common organizational structures and when to adopt them

In practice, three organizational models have gained traction:

Centralized.The entire data team is in a single area, serving the whole company. This tends to strengthen governance and reduce duplication of efforts.Decentralized.Specialists are embedded in product or business teams, favoring speed but requiring discipline to avoid silos.Hybrid.Centralized engineering with analysts and scientists close to business areas. Many organizations viewed as mature end up in this model because it combines governance and agility.

- Centralized.The entire data team is in a single area, serving the whole company. This tends to strengthen governance and reduce duplication of efforts.

- Decentralized.Specialists are embedded in product or business teams, favoring speed but requiring discipline to avoid silos.

- Hybrid.Centralized engineering with analysts and scientists close to business areas. Many organizations viewed as mature end up in this model because it combines governance and agility.

> There is no “one size fits all”! The choice depends on the maturity of the data and the strategic objectives of the organization.

## Culture and practices that make a data team thrive

In addition to roles and structures, some practices that stand out in the market as real differentiators are:

Aligning success metrics with business outcomes and not with the number of reports delivered.Documenting pipelines, metrics, and data definitions, avoiding misunderstandings and rework.Promoting fluid communication between technical teams and business areas.Investing in governance from day one with data contracts, glossary, and clear SLAs.

- Aligning success metrics with business outcomes and not with the number of reports delivered.

- Documenting pipelines, metrics, and data definitions, avoiding misunderstandings and rework.

- Promoting fluid communication between technical teams and business areas.

- Investing in governance from day one with data contracts, glossary, and clear SLAs.

## Conclusion

A well-structured data team is much more than titles in the organization. It is a combination of complementary roles. Engineering that delivers reliability, analysis that generates meaning, and science that anticipates the future. All of this driven by a data-oriented culture and continuous collaboration.

articles.title: When JSON Broke Us — and How the “VARIANT” Type in Databricks Saved Us.
articles.excerpt: “In a pipeline, a JSON brought a new and silent field — metadata.new_feature — and out of nowhere, several downstream jobs simply failed.
articles.body:
- Report this article

## Otávio R.

> “In a pipeline, a JSON brought a new and silent field —metadata.new_feature — and out of nowhere, several downstream jobs simply failed. That’s when we realized: our approach with JSON string could no longer handle evolutions.”

## Introduction / motivation.

In the real world of data engineering, we don’t always control the source of the data. Upstream systems send JSONs with variable fields, new features appear without warning, and we need to absorb this without breaking the downstream ecosystem.

For a long time, our simple strategy was: we captured the raw JSON as a string, and then we paired it when necessary. It seemed reasonable — until it became an emergency routine every time the schema varied.

In this article, I will share how we adopted the VARIANT type within Databricks / Delta Lake, how the migration went, the trade-offs we faced, and the gains that made the team lighter.

## The pipeline collapse and the invisible tension.

To illustrate, imagine this scenario (based on what we went through):

The upstream registration system starts sending a new field called tracking.sessionId in the JSONs. In the staging environment, everything passes — there is no data with this field. In production, a real JSON includes tracking.sessionId, and our job that did SELECT json_data:sessionId AS session_id fails with a parsing error. The team rushes to add columns, adjust UDFs, emergency ALTER TABLE scripts, test, revalidate. Meanwhile, dashboards become unstable. With each new customer feature, we repeat the same cycle.

- The upstream registration system starts sending a new field called tracking.sessionId in the JSONs.

- In the staging environment, everything passes — there is no data with this field.

- In production, a real JSON includes tracking.sessionId, and our job that did SELECT json_data:sessionId AS session_id fails with a parsing error.

- The team rushes to add columns, adjust UDFs, emergency ALTER TABLE scripts, test, revalidate.

- Meanwhile, dashboards become unstable. With each new customer feature, we repeat the same cycle.

The tension between software engineers and data engineers was latent:

The software engineer wanted compression, smaller payloads, to avoid sending superfluous fields. I, as a data engineer, wanted visibility, to capture all available data for future analytical uses.

- The software engineer wanted compression, smaller payloads, to avoid sending superfluous fields.

- I, as a data engineer, wanted visibility, to capture all available data for future analytical uses.

We tried hybrid approaches — extracting columns for the most used fields, leaving a “residual JSON” field as a string — but the maintenance cost, conditional parsing, UDFs, and schema evolution grew exponentially.

## The turning point: the discovery of the VARIANT type

In one of our architecture discussions, the mention of the VARIANT datatype in Delta Lake came up. I confess I had never used this feature, but upon researching, I realized it was exactly what we needed to deal with the unpredictability of MongoDB data.

## What is the VARIANT type?

VARIANT is a data type designed to store semi-structured information — such as JSON, structs, arrays, maps, or even scalar values — in a single column, without the requirement of a fixed schema.

## Internal representation.

Instead of keeping the JSON as a string and requiring constant parsing, VARIANT uses an internal binary format. This allows for more efficient navigation through the fields, reducing processing overhead and increasing query performance.

## Accessing fields.

The syntax for accessing subfields is quite natural. We can use paths like col:field, col:field.subfield or even specific functions like variant_get and try_variant_get to safely extract values.

## Inspecting content.

Another strong point is the ability to inspect the structure of the stored payload. Functions like schema_of_variant and schema_of_variant_agg allow for dynamically inferring the internal schema of a dataset, facilitating exploratory analyses and decisions on which fields to “promote” to explicit columns.

## Maturity of the feature.

It is worth noting that support for VARIANT is still in Public Preview, available from Databricks Runtime 15.3. This means that while it is fully functional, it may be subject to adjustments and improvements in future versions.

## Limitations to consider.

At the same time, the VARIANT type does not solve everything. It brings restrictions that need to be known before going into production.

Partitioning and clustering. VARIANT type columns cannot be used to partition tables, nor as clustering or Z-order keys. This means you cannot directly use the content of a VARIANT as a physical table organization strategy in Delta Lake. Databricks Documentation

Limited operations. It is not possible to apply GROUP BY, ORDER BY, DISTINCT, or set operators (UNION, INTERSECT) directly on VARIANT columns. When such operations are necessary, the solution is to extract the desired field to an explicit column before aggregation or sorting. Microsoft Learn.

Missing statistics. Delta does not collect min and max statistics on VARIANT columns. This can affect the optimizer's ability to apply efficient data skipping or pruning, impacting the performance of certain queries.

Maximum size. Each value stored in a VARIANT column has a limit of approximately 16 MiB. If a JSON document exceeds this size, it will be treated as a corrupted record.

Compatibility and protocol. When enabling VARIANT support in a table, the Delta protocol is elevated. This means that clients or tools that do not understand this new protocol (such as older versions of libraries) may no longer be able to read or write to the table.

Even with these limitations, the balance was positive in our experience: we gained flexibility without losing performance and significantly reduced the manual effort of the team to handle the constant evolution of JSON schemas.

## Migrating to VARIANT: a step-by-step guide.

Here is a migration template that can serve as a basis for you to adapt to your context:

## 1. Preparation and freezing of ingestion:

Stop or pause ingestion processes (streams, jobs) that write to the target table. Ensure that there are no concurrent writes during the migration.

- Stop or pause ingestion processes (streams, jobs) that write to the target table.

- Ensure that there are no concurrent writes during the migration.

## 2. Create intermediate table (staging).

Assuming your current schema has something like:

You can create a copy:

## Recommended by LinkedIn

## 3. Drop / rename the original table.

You can rename or drop the original table:

## 4. Create a new table with VARIANT column

If you want, you can enable VARIANT support in an existing table via:

but normally prefer to recreate. Microsoft Learning.

## 5. Copy data converting payload

This step converts the JSON string payload to VARIANT.

## Adjusting the ingestion pipelines.

After creating the table with VARIANT type support, the next step was to adapt our ingestion pipelines — both in batch and streaming — so that the data would be written in this format.

In the case of pipelines that still received JSON as a string, we applied the parse_json(...) function during the write process. This conversion ensures that the content is already persisted as VARIANT, avoiding the need for parsing in each query.

When using Auto Loader, we took advantage of the singleVariantColumn option, which allows loading the entire JSON directly into a VARIANT type column, greatly simplifying ingestion and reducing the effort of defining dynamic schemas.

Additionally, in APIs and connectors that already deliver data in JSON format, we configured them to send the payload directly to the VARIANT field, eliminating intermediate transformations.

This change brought clarity to the process: instead of maintaining different ingestion strategies to handle data variability, we centralized everything in VARIANT — gaining consistency, performance, and fewer failure points.

## 7. Validate and test.

Re-run jobs, dashboards, ETFs, and downstream pipelines. Check:

If old queries that expected to extract fields from the string still work (now using :field or variant_get) If performance is acceptable. If cases of “malformed” or very large JSONs are captured as corrupted records.

- If old queries that expected to extract fields from the string still work (now using :field or variant_get)

- If performance is acceptable.

- If cases of “malformed” or very large JSONs are captured as corrupted records.

## 8. Cleanup.

After confirming that everything works, drop the staging or old table, clean up metadata, backup if any.

This process is based on reports from users who have already adopted VARIANT. Medium+1

## Usage examples / queries with the new model.

Here are some examples that you can copy and adapt:

It is also important to work with schema_of_variant or schema_of_variant_agg:

These functions help understand the heterogeneity of the data and decide which fields to “promote” to explicit columns.

In the case of NULLs, note that there is a distinction between SQL NULL and variant NULL. Use is_variant_null(...) to differentiate. Databricks Documentation+1

## Expected results, gains, and pitfalls we encountered.

## Gains we observed:

Less pipeline breakage when new fields arise — many new attributes are absorbed into the VARIANT payload without impacting transformations. Performance improvement in queries that previously parsed JSON repeatedly. In benchmarks published by Databricks, VARIANT reached speeds ~8× faster than JSON-string. Databricks Less effort in schema migration and maintenance of ALTER TABLE scripts. Greater freedom for upstream changes — the pipeline becomes more resilient. Ability to discover emerging fields via schema_of_variant, allowing informed decisions on which fields to extract.

- Less pipeline breakage when new fields arise — many new attributes are absorbed into the VARIANT payload without impacting transformations.

- Performance improvement in queries that previously parsed JSON repeatedly. In benchmarks published by Databricks, VARIANT reached speeds ~8× faster than JSON-string. Databricks

- Less effort in schema migration and maintenance of ALTER TABLE scripts.

- Greater freedom for upstream changes — the pipeline becomes more resilient.

- Ability to discover emerging fields via schema_of_variant, allowing informed decisions on which fields to extract.

## Pitfalls and hard lessons:

Jobs that did GROUP BY payload:eventType needed to be rewritten, extracting the field beforehand, because VARIANT cannot be grouped directly. Legacy tools or clients that did not support the new table protocol began to fail. Loss of automatic statistics (min/max) for VARIANT columns affects pruning and read optimization. Very large JSON records (>16 MiB) were treated as corrupted — we had to deal with extreme cases. In cases where a field has different types across rows (e.g., number vs string), there was a type conflict: schema_of_variant returned as VARIANT instead of STRING or INT. The limitations of GROUP BY / ORDER BY / DISTINCT require you to plan ahead which fields should be extracted from the payload to explicit columns.

- Jobs that did GROUP BY payload:eventType needed to be rewritten, extracting the field beforehand, because VARIANT cannot be grouped directly.

- Legacy tools or clients that did not support the new table protocol began to fail.

- Loss of automatic statistics (min/max) for VARIANT columns affects pruning and read optimization.

- Very large JSON records (>16 MiB) were treated as corrupted — we had to deal with extreme cases.

- In cases where a field has different types across rows (e.g., number vs string), there was a type conflict: schema_of_variant returned as VARIANT instead of STRING or INT.

- The limitations of GROUP BY / ORDER BY / DISTINCT require you to plan ahead which fields should be extracted from the payload to explicit columns.

## Conclusion and recommendations for data teams.

This type of journey — from the predictable pain of JSON to the relief of VARIANT — brought us two central learnings:

Balance is essential. We did not extract everything to columns — only the fields that really matter for filters, aggregations, or joins — and left the rest in VARIANT. The hybrid model is powerful. Planning is key. Testing, controlled migration, compatibility with legacy clients, monitoring extreme payloads — all of this avoids surprises.

- Balance is essential. We did not extract everything to columns — only the fields that really matter for filters, aggregations, or joins — and left the rest in VARIANT. The hybrid model is powerful.

- Planning is key. Testing, controlled migration, compatibility with legacy clients, monitoring extreme payloads — all of this avoids surprises.

If I were to give a recommendation to those starting out:

Start with non-critical tables to experiment with VARIANT. Do comparative benchmarks JSON-string vs VARIANT. Monitor corrupted cases / large payloads. Use schema_of_variant_agg to understand which fields tend to appear. Plan incremental migrations: move to explicit columns only when truly necessary.

- Start with non-critical tables to experiment with VARIANT.

- Do comparative benchmarks JSON-string vs VARIANT.

- Monitor corrupted cases / large payloads.

- Use schema_of_variant_agg to understand which fields tend to appear.

- Plan incremental migrations: move to explicit columns only when truly necessary.

articles.title: Data Teams Are Not Bakeries.
articles.excerpt: The invisible cost of treating data as one-off orders. Imagine walking into a bakery.
articles.body:
- Report this article

## Otávio R.

> The invisible cost of treating data as one-off requests.

Imagine walking into a pastry shop. A customer places a specific order, the cook rushes to fry it, delivers quickly, and soon another order arrives, completely different. There’s no standard recipe, no planning for the month’s menu, no business strategy behind it: just orders being fulfilled, one by one. This scene, so commonplace, is a perfect metaphor for what happens in many data teams within companies.

When we treat a data team like a pastry shop, the outcome is predictable: many orders arriving at the same time, all urgent, without context or strategic alignment. The team rushes to deliver, but quality varies, errors accumulate, and executives' trust in the data diminishes. The success metric shifts to “how many orders did we fulfill this week” instead of “how much strategic value did we generate for the business.”

This criticism is not new. Sven Balnojan, in What’s Wrong With Data Teams (Medium, 2022), shows how many BI teams end up becoming “internal ticket consultancies,” always ready to fulfill requests but rarely creating strategic impact. Yuliia Tkachova, in Why do data teams need to adopt product thinking? (Medium, 2023), reinforces that only by adopting a product mindset — with ownership, roadmap, and long-term vision — can teams break this cycle. The Harvard Business Review also published, in A Better Way to Put Your Data to Work (2022), that companies need to treat data as a product to reduce redundancies and accelerate innovation.

In Brazil, the research State of Data Brazil 2024–2025 (Data Hackers + Bain) confirms this perception: a large part of teams still spends too much time on manual tasks and corrections, instead of generating strategic insights. This reinforces the pastry shop feeling — a lot of effort in meeting immediate demand, little room for innovation.

The motivation behind this metaphor is clear: to provoke reflection. If executives continue to see data as something “over the counter,” the company will never reap the benefits of true digital transformation. The invisible costs are high: rework, fragility in processes, decisions based on unreliable information, and loss of motivation among professionals who feel like mere executors.

## Recommended by LinkedIn

But there are different paths. Magazine Luiza structured its culture and data platform to integrate physical and digital operations, supporting marketing, logistics, and expansion decisions (Ilumeo, 2020). Grant Thornton Brazil transformed HR data into People Analytics assets, consolidating records and anticipating strategic analyses (Mindsight, 2023). In these examples, the teams stopped being order takers and became engines of strategy.

What differentiates these cases is the change in mindset. Instead of viewing data as one-off services, they began to treat it as strategic assets. This means creating clear roles — such as data product owners and data stewards — adopting prioritization criteria based on business value, establishing impact metrics, investing in governance and infrastructure. Above all, having executive support to sponsor the change.

Executives need to ask themselves: are we requesting “data pastries” to solve today’s specific problems or are we building data assets that will sustain our business in the future? The difference between a pastry shop and a strategic kitchen is enormous. In the former, the rush never ends. In the latter, there is planning, quality, and a long-term vision. And it is precisely this transition that will determine which companies will be able to compete in an increasingly data-driven world.

## Recommended reading:

Sven Balnojan — What’s Wrong With Data Teams (Medium, 2022):link Yuliia Tkachova — Why do data teams need to adopt product thinking? (Medium, 2023):link Desai, Fountaine & Rowshankish — A Better Way to Put Your Data to Work (Harvard Business Review, 2022):link Data Hackers & Bain — State of Data Brazil 2024–2025:link Case Magalu — Ilumeo (2020):link Case Grant Thornton Brasil — Mindsight (2023):link

- Sven Balnojan — What’s Wrong With Data Teams (Medium, 2022):link

- Yuliia Tkachova — Why do data teams need to adopt product thinking? (Medium, 2023):link

- Desai, Fountaine & Rowshankish — A Better Way to Put Your Data to Work (Harvard Business Review, 2022):link

- Data Hackers & Bain — State of Data Brazil 2024–2025:link

- Case Magalu — Ilumeo (2020):link

- Case Grant Thornton Brasil — Mindsight (2023):link

articles.title: Data Governance Automation on Databricks with LLM
articles.excerpt: Summary: This article describes a practical architecture for automating data governance on Databricks using LLMs, Unity Catalog (governed tags, automatic classification, masks and filters), AI Functions (ai_query), Vector Search, and Databricks Workflows. The roadmap was inspired by the demo 'Track 1'
articles.body:
- Report this article

## Otávio R.

> Summary: This article describes a practical architecture to automate data governance on Databricks using LLMs, Unity Catalog (governed tags, automatic classification, masks, and filters), AI Functions (ai_query), Vector Search, and Databricks Workflows. The roadmap was inspired by the demo “Track 1 – DAIWT SP 2025” and is ready for corporate adaptation focusing on LGPD/GDPR.

GitHub — Track 1 – DAIWT SP 2025 (https://github.com/databricks-solutions/databricks_demos_br/tree/main/daiwt_sp_2025/trilha1)

## 1) Context and Objectives

Effective governance in a Lakehouse requires classifying data, applying access policies, auditing usage, and measuring coverage— all continuously. LLMs reduce manual work: they can read samples of data, metadata, and glossaries to suggest standardized tags, column masks, and row filters. In Databricks, this integrates natively with Unity Catalog and orchestration with Workflows.

The goal of this guide: to deliver a blueprint that you can reproduce to:

Automatically classify data (columns, tables) and assign tags (e.g.: privacy=PII, sensitivity=high). Apply access policies (masks/filters, or ABAC when enabled) based on these tags. Audit coverage and monitor quality/compliance. Do all this in batches (daily/hourly) via Databricks Workflows.

- Automatically classify data (columns, tables) and assign tags (e.g.: privacy=PII, sensitivity=high).

- Apply access policies (masks/filters, or ABAC when enabled) based on these tags.

- Audit coverage and monitor quality/compliance.

- Do all this in batches (daily/hourly) via Databricks Workflows.

## 2) Reference Architecture

Main components

Unity Catalog (UC): unified catalog; supports governed tags, lineage, data classification via Databricks' AI engine; masks and filters. AI Functions (SQL): ai_query() function to invoke Model Serving endpoints (foundational or custom models) from SQL/Jobs. Vector Search (optional): for RAG of the governance agent (search in internal policies, glossaries, and LGPD regulations). Databricks Workflows: scheduling and dependencies (YAML job or UI) to run the steps. System Tables/Information Schema: auditing of tags, grants, lineage, access.

- Unity Catalog (UC): unified catalog; supports governed tags, lineage, data classification via Databricks' AI engine; masks and filters.

- AI Functions (SQL): ai_query() function to invoke Model Serving endpoints (foundational or custom models) from SQL/Jobs.

- Vector Search (optional): for RAG of the governance agent (search in internal policies, glossaries, and LGPD regulations).

- Databricks Workflows: scheduling and dependencies (YAML job or UI) to run the steps.

- System Tables/Information Schema: auditing of tags, grants, lineage, access.

High-level flow

Inventory→Sampling→LLM Classification→Normalization→Apply Tags→Policies→Audit & Metrics.

- Inventory→Sampling→LLM Classification→Normalization→Apply Tags→Policies→Audit & Metrics.

## 3) Prerequisites and Best Practices

Unity Catalog enabled and metastore configured. Privileges: APPLY TAG, USE CATALOG/SCHEMA, MODIFY on target tables/columns. Model Serving (foundational or custom model endpoint) accessible from the workspace. Serverless / Warehouse compatible for ai_query() (or execute on a cluster supported by the indicated Runtime). Tag standards defined: canonical keys and values (e.g.: privacy={PII,SENSITIVE,PUBLIC}, domain={financial,academic,...}). Corporate glossary (optional) in Vector Search to provide context to the LLM.

- Unity Catalog enabled and metastore configured.

- Privileges: APPLY TAG, USE CATALOG/SCHEMA, MODIFY on target tables/columns.

- Model Serving (foundational or custom model endpoint) accessible from the workspace.

- Serverless / Warehouse compatible for ai_query() (or execute on a cluster supported by the indicated Runtime).

- Tag standards defined: canonical keys and values (e.g.: privacy={PII,SENSITIVE,PUBLIC}, domain={financial,academic,...}).

- Corporate glossary (optional) in Vector Search to provide context to the LLM.

> LGPD Note: classify purpose and legal basis as metadata (tags) when applicable, and limit sampling (see Section 10) to minimize exposure of PII.

## 4) Automation Pipeline (step by step)

## 4.1 Object Inventory

List catalogs, schemas, tables, and columns without tag coverage:

## 4.2 Secure Sampling

Capture limited samples per column (e.g.: 50 values), preferring hashing/partial or synthetic data for PII. Store in a temporary table.

## 4.3 Classification with LLM via `ai_query()`

Create a SQL UDF that calls the LLM endpoint and returns a typed structure with standardized labels.

> Tip: for structured output, use responseFormat with JSON/DDL and then from_json() in SQL.

Now apply the UDF on the target columns (assuming a table with samples aggregated by column):

## 4.4 Normalization → Tags

Map class to standardized tags (can be multiple):

class=PII → privacy=PII, mask=requiredclass=SENSITIVE → privacy=SENSITIVEclass=PUBLIC → privacy=PUBLIC

- class=PII → privacy=PII, mask=required

- class=SENSITIVE → privacy=SENSITIVE

- class=PUBLIC → privacy=PUBLIC

## 4.5 Applying Tags (SQL)

Use SET TAG or ALTER ... SET TAGS to write metadata to the UC.

> Governed Tags: if using governed tags, ensure ASSIGN permissions and the allowed value set.

## 4.6 Access Policies Based on Tags

Option A – Column Mask (UDF + MASK)

Option B – ABAC (when enabled)

Define column/row policies with conditions like hasTag('privacy') = 'PII' or hasTagValue('privacy','PII') and associate with broad scopes (catalog, schema). Keep tags as the single source of truth; policies are declarative and automatically apply to new objects as tags are added.

- Define column/row policies with conditions like hasTag('privacy') = 'PII' or hasTagValue('privacy','PII') and associate with broad scopes (catalog, schema).

- Keep tags as the single source of truth; policies are declarative and automatically apply to new objects as tags are added.

## Recommended by LinkedIn

## 4.7 Audit & Metrics

Tag coverage by domain:

- Tag coverage by domain:

PII columns without mask (compliance gap):

- PII columns without mask (compliance gap):

Lineage: use the UC lineage catalog to identify producers/consumers and trigger owners.

- Lineage: use the UC lineage catalog to identify producers/consumers and trigger owners.

## 5) Implementation in Python (modular)

> Standards: PEP-8, Google docstrings; modular; idempotent. Use cluster/warehouse with appropriate permissions.

> Note: in environments with ABAC, replace the apply_mask_if_needed step with tag-oriented declarative policies at the catalog/schema level.

## 6) Orchestration with Databricks Workflows (YAML)

Example of a YAML job to run daily at 02:00, using the same cluster (or serverless) and a notebook governance_llm_job.py in the project repository:

> Tip: if you already standardize jobs with YAML (e.g.: generic_platform_core_streaming_job), keep the same cluster and just reference the parameterized governance notebook.

## 7) Observability and Governance KPIs

Tag coverage: % of tables/columns with defined privacy. Security gaps: PII columns without active mask/policy. Remediation time: median between detection and correction. Compliance by domain/squad: KPIs by owner/domain/system. Lineage-aware: prioritize widely distributed upstreams.

- Tag coverage: % of tables/columns with defined privacy.

- Security gaps: PII columns without active mask/policy.

- Remediation time: median between detection and correction.

- Compliance by domain/squad: KPIs by owner/domain/system.

- Lineage-aware: prioritize widely distributed upstreams.

Automate dashboards with System Tables and Information Schema.

## 8) Costs and Optimizations

Native automatic classification of UC can reduce costly scans. When using a custom LLM, apply sampling in batches. AI Functions: consolidate prompts, set temperature=0, reuse UDFs; evaluate token costs. Vector Search (if using RAG): choose optimized endpoints for storage to reduce cost per GB and speed up indexing. Agent Evaluation (optional): measure quality/cost/latency of the governance agent in batch.

- Native automatic classification of UC can reduce costly scans. When using a custom LLM, apply sampling in batches.

- AI Functions: consolidate prompts, set temperature=0, reuse UDFs; evaluate token costs.

- Vector Search (if using RAG): choose optimized endpoints for storage to reduce cost per GB and speed up indexing.

- Agent Evaluation (optional): measure quality/cost/latency of the governance agent in batch.

## 9) Security, LGPD, and Compliance

Minimize personal data in sampling; prefer hashing/partialization. Legal basis & purpose as tags; principle of necessity for retention. Audit accesses and record decisions (Commits/PRs in Repos) for audit trail. This is not legal advice— validate with the privacy/legal team.

- Minimize personal data in sampling; prefer hashing/partialization.

- Legal basis & purpose as tags; principle of necessity for retention.

- Audit accesses and record decisions (Commits/PRs in Repos) for audit trail.

- This is not legal advice— validate with the privacy/legal team.

## 10) Advanced Extensions

RAG for internal policies: index regulations and data standards in Vector Search so that the LLM cites the corporate reference when proposing tags/policies. Integration with DBT: synchronize DBT selectors/tags with UC tags after each deploy. ChatOps: expose an endpoint (Databricks Apps/Serving) for on-demand classification requests. ABAC (Beta): declarative masking/filtering policies oriented to hasTag() at the catalog/schema level.

- RAG for internal policies: index regulations and data standards in Vector Search so that the LLM cites the corporate reference when proposing tags/policies.

- Integration with DBT: synchronize DBT selectors/tags with UC tags after each deploy.

- ChatOps: expose an endpoint (Databricks Apps/Serving) for on-demand classification requests.

- ABAC (Beta): declarative masking/filtering policies oriented to hasTag() at the catalog/schema level.

## 11) Appendix A — Quick SQL Examples

Apply tags in batch

Query column tags

Example of a mask function with parameter

## 12) Appendix B — Base prompt `ai_query()`

## Conclusion

With just a few native components from Databricks, you can close the governance cycle with LLM: inventory, classify, apply policies, and audit — all automated, reproducible, and measurable. Adjust the granularity (column/table), refine the prompt, and promote the use of governed tags as a common language among engineering, security, and legal.

articles.title: Prejudice in the Use of AI!
articles.excerpt: Prejudice in the use of Artificial Intelligence (AI), also known as algorithmic bias, refers to the tendency of AI systems to produce discriminatory outcomes due to human biases present in the training data or in the algorithm's design. This issue is crucial, as AI systems
articles.body:
- Report this article

## Otávio R.

Prejudice in the use of Artificial Intelligence (AI), also known as algorithmic bias, refers to the tendency of AI systems to produce discriminatory results due to human biases present in the training data or the algorithm's design. This issue is crucial, as biased AI systems can lead to unfair decisions, discrimination, and the perpetuation of social inequalities, impacting areas such as hiring, criminal justice, and healthcare. The relevance of this topic lies in its ability to amplify existing social problems, making a multidisciplinary approach essential for its mitigation.

## Types of Bias in AI

Biases in AI can be categorized in various ways, based on their origins and impacts. Below, we present a classification based on recent research:

Data Bias: Occurs when the data used to train the AI is unrepresentative or contains historical biases. For example, if the training data for a facial recognition system predominantly includes faces of white people, the system may perform poorly for other ethnicities. Algorithmic Bias: Refers to biases introduced by the algorithm itself or by the choice of model, such as the selection of features that favor certain groups. This can happen, for example, in credit granting algorithms that prioritize variables correlated with socioeconomic privileges. Emergent Bias: Arises from the interaction between the system and its users, reflecting social dynamics that were not present in the initial data. Evaluation Bias: Occurs when the model validation does not consider diverse groups, masking biases in specific subgroups.

- Data Bias: Occurs when the data used to train the AI is unrepresentative or contains historical biases. For example, if the training data for a facial recognition system predominantly includes faces of white people, the system may perform poorly for other ethnicities.

- Algorithmic Bias: Refers to biases introduced by the algorithm itself or by the choice of model, such as the selection of features that favor certain groups. This can happen, for example, in credit granting algorithms that prioritize variables correlated with socioeconomic privileges.

- Emergent Bias: Arises from the interaction between the system and its users, reflecting social dynamics that were not present in the initial data.

- Evaluation Bias: Occurs when the model validation does not consider diverse groups, masking biases in specific subgroups.

These categories help identify where and how bias can infiltrate the AI lifecycle, from problem definition to implementation.

## Practical Examples and Consequences

Real-world examples illustrate how bias in AI can have significant impacts. A notable case is Amazon's hiring algorithm, developed in 2014, which was discontinued in 2015 after it was found to discriminate against female candidates, reflecting historical hiring patterns that were predominantly male (Amazon’s AI Recruiting Tool). Another example is the facial recognition system, which often shows higher error rates for people with darker skin, due to under-representation in training data sets (Face Recognition Bias).

The consequences are broad:

Social: Perpetuates stereotypes and discrimination, deepening racial and gender inequalities.

- Social: Perpetuates stereotypes and discrimination, deepening racial and gender inequalities.

Economic: Results in unfair hiring and credit scoring practices, limiting opportunities for marginalized groups. Ethical: Violates principles of justice and equity, eroding public trust in AI technologies.

- Economic: Results in unfair hiring and credit scoring practices, limiting opportunities for marginalized groups.

- Ethical: Violates principles of justice and equity, eroding public trust in AI technologies.

A study from USC Viterbi School of Engineering found that up to 38.6% of the “facts” used by AI knowledge bases, such as GenericsKB, may be biased, highlighting the extent of the problem (USC Bias Study).

## Mitigation Strategies

Mitigating bias in AI requires a systematic approach at all stages of development and deployment. Below, we detail strategies based on recent research:

Diverse and Representative Data Collection: Ensure that training data includes a wide range of demographics, avoiding under-representation. For example, facial recognition data sets should include faces of various ethnicities and genders. Bias Detection and Audit Tools: Utilize methods such as bias detection algorithms (e.g., AIF360, AIF360 Tool) to identify disparities in model outcomes. Justice-Conscious Algorithms: Develop models that incorporate fairness metrics, such as equal opportunity or equalized odds, to reduce disparities in protected groups. Transparency and Explainability: Make AI systems transparent, allowing decisions to be explained, helping to identify and correct biases. Explainability techniques, such as “model cards” and “data sheets,” are recommended (Model Cards). Ethical Guidelines and Regulations: Establish ethical standards, such as those proposed by the High-Level Expert Group on AI of the EU, and regulations that require regular audits of AI systems (EU AI Ethics Guidelines). Continuous Monitoring and Evaluation: Regularly assess AI performance across different groups, adjusting the model as necessary to avoid amplifying biases over time.

- Diverse and Representative Data Collection: Ensure that training data includes a wide range of demographics, avoiding under-representation. For example, facial recognition data sets should include faces of various ethnicities and genders.

- Bias Detection and Audit Tools: Utilize methods such as bias detection algorithms (e.g., AIF360, AIF360 Tool) to identify disparities in model outcomes.

- Justice-Conscious Algorithms: Develop models that incorporate fairness metrics, such as equal opportunity or equalized odds, to reduce disparities in protected groups.

- Transparency and Explainability: Make AI systems transparent, allowing decisions to be explained, helping to identify and correct biases. Explainability techniques, such as “model cards” and “data sheets,” are recommended (Model Cards).

- Ethical Guidelines and Regulations: Establish ethical standards, such as those proposed by the High-Level Expert Group on AI of the EU, and regulations that require regular audits of AI systems (EU AI Ethics Guidelines).

- Continuous Monitoring and Evaluation: Regularly assess AI performance across different groups, adjusting the model as necessary to avoid amplifying biases over time.

A McKinsey report suggests six essential steps, including staying updated on research and using internal teams (“red teams”) to identify biases (McKinsey Bias Mitigation).

## Future Perspectives and Challenges

The future of combating bias in AI involves significant challenges, such as the lack of diverse data in many domains, the complexity of defining fairness in varied contexts (for example, definitions of fairness may differ among communities, as discussed in Justice Definitions), and the trade-off between accuracy and fairness. Future research should prioritize:

Development of robust methods for bias detection and correction, such as mitigation algorithms (Bias Mitigation Research). Creation of standardized benchmarks to assess fairness, facilitating comparisons between systems. Integration of fairness considerations throughout the AI lifecycle, from problem definition to deployment. Education of developers and users about the impacts of bias in AI, promoting an ethical and inclusive approach.

- Development of robust methods for bias detection and correction, such as mitigation algorithms (Bias Mitigation Research).

- Creation of standardized benchmarks to assess fairness, facilitating comparisons between systems.

- Integration of fairness considerations throughout the AI lifecycle, from problem definition to deployment.

- Education of developers and users about the impacts of bias in AI, promoting an ethical and inclusive approach.

## AI Development Stages and Possible Biases

Below is a table detailing where bias can infiltrate at each stage of AI development, based on research:

This table can be included in the article to organize the discussion about sources of bias.

## Conclusion

Bias in the use of AI is a multifaceted problem that requires ongoing attention from developers, policymakers, and society. By integrating mitigation strategies and promoting future research, we can work towards fairer and more equitable AI systems. This report provides a solid foundation for your article, with examples, data, and references that can be explored for rich and informative content.

Key Citations

What Do We Do About the Biases in AI Harvard Business Review article How AI Bias Really Happens MIT Technology Review Portuguese article Bias in artificial intelligence algorithms and recommendations for mitigate PMC open access research paper Mitigating Bias in AI Algorithms Toptal guide for developers AI Bias Examples IBM think piece with real-world cases Amazon’s AI Recruiting Tool Is No Longer in Use After It Discriminated Against Women Reuters news article Face Recognition Is Accurate, If You’re a White Guy Technology Review article on facial recognition bias Machine Bias ProPublica investigation into criminal justice algorithms Bias in AI Examples and 6 Ways to Fix it in 2025 AIMultiple research article USC Bias Study USC Viterbi School of Engineering research on AI data bias AIF360 Tool IBM AI Fairness 360 open-source toolkit for bias detection Model Cards for Model Reporting AIES conference paper on AI transparency EU AI Ethics Guidelines European Commission guidelines for trustworthy AI McKinsey Bias Mitigation McKinsey insights on tackling AI and human biases Bias Mitigation Research AIES conference paper on bias mitigation strategies Justice Definitions Washington Post analysis on algorithmic fairness Jornal USP IA Bias Jornal da USP article on AI reflecting biases and inequalities Gizmodo Brasil Study Gizmodo Brazil article on AI racial and gender biases Olhar Digital IA Olhar Digital article on AI perpetuating social inequalities

articles.title: A New Industrial Revolution and Its Impact on the Job Market
articles.excerpt: Introduction Did you know that by 2030, artificial intelligence (AI) could add up to $15.7 trillion to the global economy? That's more than the combined GDP of China and India, according to a study by PwC (https://www.pwc.
articles.body:
- Report this article

## Otávio R.

## Introduction

Did you know that by 2030, artificial intelligence (AI) could add up to $15.7 trillion to the global economy? That's more than the combined GDP of China and India, according to a study by PwC (https://www.pwc.com/gx/en/issues/artificial-intelligence/publications/artificial-intelligence-study.html). This impressive figure not only reflects the economic potential of AI but also points to a radical transformation in the job market and the global economy — a change that echoes the great industrial revolutions of the past. Just as the steam engine propelled the First Industrial Revolution, AI is redefining the present and future of work. However, unlike previous revolutions, this new era brings a speed, scale, and unique challenges that we cannot ignore.

## A New Industrial Revolution

Industrial revolutions have been pivotal moments in history. The first brought mechanization, the second introduced electricity and mass production, and the third gave us automation and computing. Now, we are living what many call the fourth industrial revolution, driven by AI and digitalization. Just like in past eras, we see machines replacing human tasks — but this time, not only physical tasks but also cognitive ones.

What sets this revolution apart is the accelerated pace. While previous transformations took decades to solidify, AI is advancing exponentially, reshaping markets in just a few years. An example of this is the adoption of generative AI: ChatGPT, for instance, reached 100 million users in less than a year, surpassing the adoption speed of technologies like smartphones and tablets, according to data from eMarketer AI Adoption (https://www.emarketer.com/content/generative-ai-adoption-climbed-faster-than-smartphones-tablets). Sectors such as healthcare, finance, manufacturing, and services are already being impacted, with algorithms optimizing processes, personalizing experiences, and even making complex decisions.

## Recommended by LinkedIn

## Challenges and Opportunities

Changes of this magnitude bring significant challenges. One of the most urgent is the reskilling of the workforce. The IMF estimates that nearly 40% of global employment is exposed to AI, with up to 60% of jobs in advanced economies potentially affected (International Monetary Fund AI Economy Impact (https://www.imf.org/en/Blogs/Articles/2024/01/14/ai-will-transform-the-global-economy-lets-make-sure-it-benefits-humanity)). This means that professions that once seemed secure, such as data analysis or even medicine, now face automation. A report from the McKinsey Global Institute suggests that up to 375 million workers may need to change occupations by 2030, while the World Economic Forum projects the creation of 97 million new jobs and the elimination of 85 million by 2025 (Shaping the Future of Work, a WEF Global Shaper Initiative (https://www.weforum.org/reports/the-future-of-jobs-report-2018)). This requires a swift response in terms of education and training, focusing on skills that complement AI, such as critical thinking and creativity.

Moreover, ethical issues — such as biases in algorithms and data privacy — require attention. A study from Harvard University highlights that AI systems can replicate social biases, such as in hiring decisions or criminal justice, due to biased training data (Harvard University AI Ethics (https://news.harvard.edu/gazette/story/2020/10/ethical-concerns-mount-as-ai-takes-bigger-decision-making-role/)). Regulation, such as the GDPR in Europe, seeks to mitigate these risks, but the debate on how to balance innovation and ethics continues.

On the other hand, the opportunities are enormous. AI can help solve complex global problems, such as climate change, by optimizing sustainable solutions, like developing more efficient materials for wind turbines (Worth AI Climate (https://worth.com/how-ai-can-tackle-5-global-challenges-2/)). In healthcare, AI analysis of medical images is already improving diagnostics, and in agriculture, autonomous machines are increasing efficiency and food security (IABAC AI for Good (https://iabac.org/blog/ai-for-good-tackling-global-challenges-with-artificial-intelligence)). For companies, strategically integrating AI is not just a matter of survival but a chance to innovate, create new business models, and capture emerging markets.

## Conclusion

As we navigate this new era, we need to embrace change with an open and proactive mindset. Investing in our development, collaborating across sectors, and ensuring that AI is used ethically and responsibly are fundamental steps. Just as the industrial revolutions of the past shaped the world we know, the era of AI will define the future — and it is in our hands, as professionals and leaders, to make that future a positive and inclusive one.

articles.title: The Pillar of Data Governance and Reliability!
articles.excerpt: If you work with data, you know that understanding where it comes from, how it is transformed, and where it goes is essential to ensure quality, reliability, and effective auditing. This is where data lineage comes in.
articles.body:
- Report this article

## Otávio R.

If you work with data, you know that understanding where it comes from, how it is transformed, and where it goes is essential to ensure quality, reliability, and effective auditing. This is where data lineage comes in.

Imagine this scenario: a critical company report presents inconsistent numbers. Your team spends days trying to trace the source of the problem, analyzing various tables, pipelines, and transformations. In the end, they discover that a simple change in an upstream script impacted the final metric. If the data lineage had been well documented, the diagnosis would have taken minutes, not days.

## 🏆 Benefits of Data Lineage

1️⃣ Governance and Compliance— Regulations like LGPD and GDPR require data traceability. With well-structured lineage, responding to audits becomes simple and quick.

2️⃣ Debugging and Error Resolution— Bugs in ETLs and discrepancies in reports can be resolved more efficiently by understanding the entire data flow.

3️⃣ Performance Optimization— Identifying bottlenecks and eliminating unnecessary transformations improves pipeline efficiency and reduces computational costs.

4️⃣ Impact Analysis— Before modifying a pipeline or removing a table, it is crucial to know which processes will be affected.

## 🔍 Implementing Data Lineage in Databricks

The Unity Catalog brings a native approach to track and visualize data lineage in real-time within Databricks, allowing:

✅ To visualize the source and destination of data in tables, columns, and notebooks.

✅ To track which jobs, users, and processes accessed or modified the data.

## Recommended by LinkedIn

✅ Easily audit for compliance and security.

## 💡 Practical Example in Unity Catalog

Suppose your company has an ETL pipeline that reads raw data from a bucket in Azure Data Lake and transforms it into Delta tables. With the Unity Catalog, you can automatically visualize:

🔹 Which source fed the table (e.g., raw_data.customers)?

🔹 Which processes performed intermediate transformations?

🔹 Who accessed or altered the final data?

This reduces the risk of unforeseen impacts and accelerates decision-making based on reliable data.

## 🎯 Conclusion.

If you are a data professional, knowing and using data lineage is no longer optional. Tools like the Unity Catalog make this practice more accessible, efficient, and integrated into the Databricks ecosystem.

Want to improve data governance in your company? Start with lineage! 🔗💡

articles.title: The “PROMPT” Framework for AI and Its Benefits.
articles.excerpt: The PROMPT framework (Persona, Script, Objective, Model, Overview, Transform) was developed to guide the creation of clear and effective instructions for Artificial Intelligences. Below, I present each step, along with practical application examples.
articles.body:
- Report this article

## Otávio R.

The structurePROMPT(Persona, Script, Objective, Model, Context, Transform) was developed to guide the creation of clear and effective instructions for Artificial Intelligences. Below, I present each step along with practical application examples.

## 👨💻 Persona:

In this phase, we describe the role or identity that the AI should assume. For example:

> “You are an experienced marketing consultant with extensive knowledge in social media campaigns.”

By defining the persona, we ensure that the tone of the response is appropriate for the requested role.

## 🎬 Script:

Here, we define the narrative structure or step-by-step of the content. Example:

> “I want you to create a script that includes an introduction, development, and conclusion, aimed at a blog post.”

This guides the AI in organizing ideas, allowing for greater coherence.

## 🎯 Objective:

Determines the purpose of the request. Example:

> “Our goal is to engage the audience to subscribe to our email list.”

With a clear objective, the AI can align each step of the content to achieve it.

## 👕 Model:

At this point, we define the output format. Example:

> “Structure the result in 5 short topics, with tips and practical examples in each.”

This helps the AI present the information clearly and organized.

## 🌍 Context:

Provides the context and challenges of the target audience. Example:

> “The client is an entrepreneur who needs more reach on social media but is limited in budget.”

This context enriches the response with specific solutions for the presented scenario.

## 🌀 Transform:

Finally, we review and refine. It’s the time to suggest adjustments. Example:

> “Change the formal language to informal, include visual references, and add a call to action at the end.”

This step ensures that the final response is polished and perfectly meets the desired outcome.

In my opinion, thePROMPT offers an effective guide for generating clear and objective instructions, enhancing the quality of the responses provided by the AI. Following these six steps brings gains in clarity and cohesion, benefiting both creators and consumers of the content.

articles.title: Prompt Engineering — The New Frontier in Interaction with AI
articles.excerpt: With the exponential advancement of artificial intelligence systems, prompt engineering emerges as an essential practice for optimizing the interaction between humans and machines. More than just formulating commands, this approach involves the creation, refinement, and application of instructions.
articles.body:
- Report this article

## Otávio R.

With the exponential advancement of artificial intelligence systems, prompt engineering emerges as an essential practice for optimizing the interaction between humans and machines. More than simply formulating commands, this approach involves the creation, refinement, and application of precise instructions, guiding AI to provide responses that meet the specific demands of users.

## History and Evolution

The evolution of language models has radically transformed the way we interact with technology. What began with basic commands has evolved into sophisticated techniques that require deep knowledge of semantics and syntax, consolidating prompt engineering as a strategic and indispensable skill.

## How to Learn

To enter this field, there is a variety of online courses and specializations in artificial intelligence and natural language processing. Participating in communities, webinars, and hackathons is equally important, as it allows for the exchange of experiences and the practical application of learned concepts.

## Activities and Applications

The prompt engineering professional works on the design, testing, and optimization of interactions with language models. This practice is fundamental in areas such as customer service, data analysis, and process automation, where precision in communication with AI can transform operational efficiency and innovation.

## Challenges and Limitations

Despite its great potential, prompt engineering faces challenges such as command ambiguity and misinterpretation by AI. Ethical issues, such as the misuse of data and response manipulation, also require constant attention. These barriers highlight the need for continuous updating and a critical approach.

## Recommended by LinkedIn

## Interdisciplinarity

This field combines knowledge from linguistics, technology, and data analysis, requiring a multidisciplinary perspective to create robust and adaptable solutions. This integration is crucial for developing increasingly precise and efficient interactions with AI systems.

## Resources and Communities

Various resources are available for those interested, from specialized courses to forums, conferences, and academic publications. Engaging in these communities allows one to keep up with best practices and the latest trends, as well as fostering networking with other professionals in the field.

## Future Trends and Compensation Value

As technology evolves, the demand for professionals specialized in prompt engineering is expected to grow, reflecting in increasingly competitive compensation. Technology companies and startups already recognize this skill as a strategic differentiator, projecting a promising scenario for professionals in the field.

## Conclusion

Investing in knowledge of prompt engineering is undoubtedly strategic for those looking to stand out in an increasingly innovation-driven market. Mastering this skill can be the differentiator that boosts efficiency and competitiveness across various sectors, paving the way for more precise and effective interaction with artificial intelligence.

articles.title: How Databricks is Transforming Data Management and AI
articles.excerpt: Recently, I had the opportunity to deepen my knowledge about Databricks, a platform that is revolutionizing the way organizations work with data, analytics, and artificial intelligence. Throughout the process, I learned valuable lessons that I would like to share in a practical way and
articles.body:
- Report this article

## Otávio R.

Recently, I had the opportunity to deepen my knowledge about Databricks, a platform that is revolutionizing the way organizations work with data, analytics, and artificial intelligence. During the process, I learned valuable lessons that I would like to share in a practical and objective manner.

## Key Benefits of Databricks

Databricks is a platform that combines scalability and efficiency to transform how data is managed. Among the main benefits, I highlight:

• Databricks democratizes the use of data and AI, allowing different areas of the company to collaborate more efficiently.

• It offers an end-to-end solution for implementing and managing artificial intelligence models.

• The storage is flexible and ready to handle growing volumes of data, optimizing costs and performance.

## Governance and Security with Unity Catalog

One of the major challenges in data projects is ensuring security and compliance. Unity Catalog addresses this issue by providing:

• A single solution that works across different clouds and workloads.

• Users can access only what is relevant to their roles, protecting sensitive data.

## Core Technologies

Databricks stands out for combining innovation and usability in advanced features, such as:

• Delta Lake: a storage format that brings reliability to data, merging the flexibility of data lakes with the consistency of data warehouses.

• Photon: an optimized execution engine for processing large volumes of data quickly and efficiently.

• Delta Live Tables: a declarative tool for creating continuous data pipelines, ideal for scenarios that require scalability and high performance.

## Machine Learning and AI

For those working with AI, Databricks offers a robust environment with features that include:

• Through MLflow, it is possible to track, tune, and deploy models efficiently.

• An ideal resource for advanced AI workloads.

• Lakehouse Monitoring ensures that AI workloads operate optimally and with high availability.

## Orchestration and Automation

In addition to its analytical capabilities, Databricks Workflows facilitates the automation of complex workflows, while Databricks SQL offers an intuitive experience for users who need a scalable data warehousing solution.

## Final Reflection

My learning journey with Databricks reinforced how transformative this platform can be. It serves companies that are just starting to work with data as well as those that already have sophisticated AI and Machine Learning operations. With its flexibility, security, and scalability, Databricks is an essential tool for organizations looking to leverage the value of data.
